coredata.dc:description: In this study, nitrogen- and phosphorus-co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,P-CQDs) were synthesized via a green hydrothermal method using Phyllanthus emblica for the sensitive detection of dibutyl phthalate (DBP) in plastic-packaged milk products. This research aimed to develop a sustainable, cost-effective sensing platform for monitoring food safety. The synthesized N,P-CQDs exhibited distinctive fluorescence properties with a quantum yield (QY) of 36.65 % and were characterized using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and UV–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis). These N,P-CQDs showed stable fluorescence, good resistance to changes in pH, and the ability to adapt to changes in ionic strength. This made them ideal for detecting DBP in food materials. A fluorescence quenching assay revealed a broad linear response in the 1–25 μM range and achieved a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.632 μM. Fluorescence lifetime analysis and FTIR studies confirmed a dynamic quenching mechanism driven by the inner filter effect (IFE) between DBP and N,P-CQDs. This fluorescence-based approach offers a more cost-effective, rapid, and environmentally friendly alternative than conventional DBP detection methods such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS). Recovery experiments conducted in spiked food matrices (milk, cheese, and yogurt) demonstrated recovery rates ranging from 97.60 % to 101.67 %, further validating the method's reliability in complex food samples. This method provides a reliable, sustainable, and cost-effective strategy for DBP detection, ensuring food safety and environmental monitoring while addressing the limitations of conventional analytical techniques.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, nitrogen- and phosphorus-co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,P-CQDs) were synthesized via a green hydrothermal method using Phyllanthus emblica for the sensitive detection of dibutyl phthalate (DBP) in plastic-packaged milk products. This research aimed to develop a sustainable, cost-effective sensing platform for monitoring food safety. The synthesized N,P-CQDs exhibited distinctive fluorescence properties with a quantum yield (QY) of 36.65 % and were characterized using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and UV–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis). These N,P-CQDs showed stable fluorescence, good resistance to changes in pH, and the ability to adapt to changes in ionic strength. This made them ideal for detecting DBP in food materials. A fluorescence quenching assay revealed a broad linear response in the 1–25 μM range and achieved a low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.632 μM. Fluorescence lifetime analysis and FTIR studies confirmed a dynamic quenching mechanism driven by the inner filter effect (IFE) between DBP and N,P-CQDs. This fluorescence-based approach offers a more cost-effective, rapid, and environmentally friendly alternative than conventional DBP detection methods such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and gas chromatography–mass spectrometry (GC–MS). Recovery experiments conducted in spiked food matrices (milk, cheese, and yogurt) demonstrated recovery rates ranging from 97.60 % to 101.67 %, further validating the method's reliability in complex food samples. This method provides a reliable, sustainable, and cost-effective strategy for DBP detection, ensuring food safety and environmental monitoring while addressing the limitations of conventional analytical techniques.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dibutyl phthalate (DBP) is typically used in industries such as plastics, lubricants, cosmetics, textiles, and pesticides because it makes the material harder and more pliable (Berlina et al., 2023). However, the widespread use of DBP as a food packaging component poses more significant risks to human health due to potential migration into foods, thereby exposing humans to such substances. DBP has been linked to serious health risks such as endocrine disorders, liver and urinary dysfunctions, cardiovascular diseases, reproductive problems, and even toxic conditions that can be carcinogenic, mutagenic, and teratogenic in potential (Bi et al., 2023; Wang, Lv, et al., 2023). As a plasticizer, DBP decreases the glass transition temperature of polymers, enhancing elasticity. However, its weak binding within polymer matrices leads to instability under high temperatures or pressures, facilitating DBP migration into consumables (Wang, Liu, Liang, et al., 2022). It intensifies the leaching process of DBP given its low molecular weight, making it easily penetrate food and environmental matrices with the consequent result causing observable contamination in items of food, waterways, and industrial waste (Nikova et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2023). Infants are the most vulnerable to DBP exposure. Therefore, the WHO and EPA have set strict values for limits regarding DBP levels in consumables at 25 μg/kg and 20 μg/kg of body weight, respectively (Habschied et al., 2023).

Given the toxicity of DBP, its detection in food and environmental samples is essential for human health. As shown earlier, though traditional methods like mass spectrometry, HPLC, GC, and AAS represent a sensitivity and specificity scale at a high level, they also require demands of high cost, technical skills, and instrumentation complexity (Różańska et al., 2023; Wang, Lv, et al., 2023). Fluorescence analysis presents a promising alternative to sensitive yet cost-effective detection.

Carbon quantum dots from natural origins forestall environmental issues while following the “green chemistry” principle of converting waste into valuable nanomaterials. CQDs have exceptional optical properties, a relatively lower cost, and a highly reproducible fluorescence response, making them suitable for extensive applications in fields such as bioimaging, cellular imaging, food safety detection, and electrocatalytic processes (Dong et al., 2021; Jing et al., 2022; Li, Wang, et al., 2023; Mitra et al., 2019; Qu et al., 2020; Sajwan et al., 2024; Thakur, Bains, Inbaraj, et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2024; Zhu et al., 2023; Rishabh et al., 2025). However, CQDs are synthesized by traditional methods, often showing reduced fluorescence efficiency, where only surface engineering improves their performance. Incorporating heteroatoms, particularly nitrogen N, is highly effective in enhancing the fluorescence characteristics of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) (Liao et al., 2021). The incorporation of other elements, like boron (B), sulfur (S), and phosphorus (P), incorporates functional groups that augment the detection abilities of CQDs even more (Deswal et al., 2020; Kadian & Manik, 2020; Kalaiyarasan et al., 2020, b; Rishabh et al., 2024a). While CQDs experience these advantages, the ability of Phyllanthus emblica-based CQDs for DBP detection has not been explored so far. This waste-derived resource becomes an excellent precursor for CQD synthesis due to its vast annual global production of 1.27 million metric tonnes (Mahajan et al., 2023) and high carbon content.

This research addresses the critical need for effective and sustainable methods to detect harmful plasticizers such as DBP in food products. DBP contamination in plastic-packaged goods poses significant risks to human health, including endocrine disruption and potential carcinogenic effects. Consequently, developing rapid, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly detection methods is imperative for food safety and public health protection. This study employs fluorescence spectroscopy to identify DBP with high sensitivity and selectivity in complex food matrices, ensuring accurate quantification (Dhandapani et al., 2023). The novel N,P-CQD probe, synthesized from Phyllanthus emblica waste material, represents an innovative contribution to the emerging field of liquid-phase fluorescence sensors. This approach aligns with sustainable practices by utilizing plant-based waste, reducing environmental impact while enhancing analytical performance. The research aims to evaluate the selectivity and sensitivity of N,P-CQDs for DBP detection while exploring their broader applications in food safety, agriculture, and environmental monitoring. This study contributes significantly to sustainable quality control practices and improved public health protection by providing a reliable method for detecting plasticizer contamination.

Freshly plucked Phyllanthus emblica fruits were directly obtained from the fruit tree. Dibutyl phthalate (DBP), butyl benzyl phthalate (BBP), diethyl phthalate (DEP), bis(2-ethylhexyl) 3,4,5,6-tetrabromo phthalate (TBPH), polyethylene glycol (PEG), and phthalic anhydride (PA) from Sigma-Aldrich (USA) were used. Deionized water was used as the primary solvent throughout all experimental procedures. Other solvents, such as methanol, acetonitrile (ACN), and dichloromethane, were used as additional solvent media during the experimental processes.

Phyllanthus emblica fruits were extensively washed with deionized water to remove surface contamination. Following fruit peeling, 2 g of the pulp was manually ground into a thick paste using a mortar and pestle. To achieve nitrogen and phosphorus doping, 0.6 g of urea, dissolved in 2 mL of deionized water, and 0.2 mL of orthophosphoric acid were added to the fruit paste with uniform mixing. Subsequently, 30 mL of deionized water was added, forming a homogeneous slurry. This mixture was then transferred into a hydrothermal reactor and heated in a hot air oven at 180 °C for 8 h (Tafese et al., 2023). After cooling to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min, followed by filtration using a 0.22 μm membrane filter. The filtrate was then subjected to dialysis in deionized water for 48 h to ensure complete purification. The final N,P-CQDs solution was stored at 4 °C for further application (Fig. 1 ).

To assess the fluorescence (FL) sensitivity of N,P-CQDs towards plasticizers, 1 mL of 25 μM plasticizer solution was added to 25 μL of the N,P-CQDs solution (Rishabh et al., 2024b). Following a 15-min incubation period, fluorescence intensities were measured. The experiment was conducted by varying the concentration of DBP from 1 to 25 μM under identical conditions to evaluate the concentration-dependent fluorescence response. To mimic real-world scenarios, DBP-spiked samples of plastic-packed milk, cheese, and yogurt were prepared at 5, 10, and 15 μM concentrations. The N,P-CQDs solution was then added to these samples, followed by fluorescence measurement under consistent conditions to evaluate the sensing efficiency of N,P-CQDs in food matrices.

The TEM images (Fig. 2 ) provide a detailed analysis of the N,P-CQDs' morphology and structural characteristics. Fig. 2a, captured at a 100 nm scale, reveals uniformly dispersed N,P-CQDs with minimal aggregation, suggesting effective stabilization by surface functional groups. In contrast, Fig. 2b, focused on a 20 nm range, highlights individual N,P-CQDs exhibiting a well-defined spherical morphology and a narrow size distribution, confirming the successful synthesis of quantum dots. These observations suggest that while aggregation is evident on a larger scale, the CQDs maintain their distinct morphology and homogeneity at the individual level, demonstrating the reliability of the synthesis method. Additionally, the measured average size of N,P-CQDs is approximately 6.05 nm (Fig. 2c), which aligns with the expected nanoscale dimensions for CQDs and facilitates quantum confinement effects, enhancing their fluorescence properties. The HR-TEM images provide further structural insights, indicating the presence of lattice fringes with an interlayer spacing of ∼0.34 nm, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon. This confirms the partially crystalline nature of N,P-CQDs. Furthermore, the presence of lattice distortions suggests that heteroatom doping influences the local electronic structure, which can modulate optical properties. The EDS spectra for N,P-CQDs validated the existence of carbon (C), oxygen (O), nitrogen (N), and phosphorus (P) elements, confirming successful doping. The quantitative EDS analysis revealed an elemental composition of 47.03 wt% carbon, 3.70 wt% nitrogen, 41.28 wt% oxygen, and 7.99 wt% phosphorus, further supporting the incorporation of heteroatoms into the carbon framework. The dominance of nitrogen and phosphorus peaks in Fig. 2d indicates effective doping within the carbon matrix, which contributes to altered electronic states, enhanced fluorescence properties, and improved sensing capabilities.

The chemical composition and bonding states of the N,P-CQDs were elucidated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), which confirms the successful co-doping of nitrogen and phosphorus. The survey spectrum (Fig. 3a) showed four distinguishable peaks corresponding to N 1 s, C 1 s, O 1 s, and P 2p, validating the presence of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and phosphorus in the synthesized quantum dots. The dominant C 1 s peak (284–287 eV) represents the carbonaceous nature of quantum dots, with carbon as the principal element forming the graphitic core structure. The presence of peaks at 531 eV (O 1 s), 401.2 eV (N 1 s), and 133 eV (P 2p) further confirms the incorporation of nitrogen and phosphorus dopants, which alter the electronic environment of the CQDs.

Nitrogen doping significantly influences the electronic structure, enhancing the charge transfer properties and optical behavior of the CQDs (Rani, Rishabh, Shanker, & Sillanpää, 2024). Simultaneously, oxygen-containing functional groups (CO, CO) improve surface reactivity, increasing hydrophilicity and biocompatibility (Guo et al., 2022; Yadav et al., 2023). The sharp P 2p peak at 133.5 eV is assigned to PO bonds (Fig. 3b), possibly due to phosphate groups. This indicates the incorporation of extra active sites and electronic changes in the CQDs, which can significantly improve their performance in environmental sensing and catalytic applications (Chai et al., 2021; Kalaiyarasan et al., 2020, b; Luo et al., 2023; Preethi et al., 2022; Wang, Liu, Nong, et al., 2022).

The O 1 s spectrum in Fig. 3c shows a distinct peak at 532 eV, corresponding to CO and CO bonds, verifying oxygen-containing functionalities that improve solubility and stability in aqueous media (Castorina et al., 2019). The C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3d) reveals peaks corresponding to sp2-hybridized carbon (284.6 eV), CO (287.2 eV), and CN (285.7 eV) bonding. The presence of CN and CO groups confirm successful nitrogen doping, which modifies the electronic structure, enhancing the optical and sensing capabilities of the CQDs (Blume et al., 2015). Additionally, partial oxidation during synthesis introduces surface functional groups that contribute to improved fluorescence efficiency and biocompatibility (Crispi et al., 2023; Kotronia et al., 2021).

The high-resolution N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3e) further supports nitrogen incorporation, showing deconvoluted peaks at 399.8 eV (pyridinic N) and 401.2 eV (graphitic N). Pyridinic N is known to enhance electron transfer and catalytic activity, while graphitic N contributes to photoluminescence enhancement and structural stability (Cano et al., 2019). This dual role of nitrogen doping strengthens the sensing efficiency and fluorescence properties of N,P-CQDs, making them highly suitable for bioimaging and analytical applications. Overall, XPS analysis provides strong evidence of successful nitrogen and phosphorus co-doping, which significantly modifies the electrical, optical, and surface properties of CQDs. These findings establish N,P-CQDs as highly versatile materials for sensing, catalysis, and bioimaging applications (Huang et al., 2019; Wang, Pan, Liu, et al., 2022).

The XRD spectra of N,P-CQDs, shown in Fig. 4a, display a distinct broad peak near 24.2°, attributed to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon, confirming their carbonaceous nature. The broad and diffuse nature of this peak signifies the amorphous structure of N,P-CQDs, which is a characteristic feature of carbon quantum dots. This structural disorder arises from quantum confinement effects, surface functionalization, and heteroatom doping, which disrupt long-range crystallinity. The lack of sharp peaks indicates restricted long-range order, demonstrating the disordered configuration of sp2-hybridized C atoms within the carbon framework. The introduction of nitrogen and phosphorus dopants likely induces additional lattice distortions, which further contribute to the amorphous character of N,P-CQDs. This observation is consistent with prior research on carbon-based quantum dots, where heteroatom doping modifies the electronic structure and enhances optical properties (Li et al., 2021; Verma et al., 2021).

The FT-IR spectra of N,P-CQDs (Fig. 4b) confirm the presence of various surface functional groups, which play a crucial role in their hydrophilicity, chemical reactivity, and fluorescence properties. The broad absorption bands at 3100–3450 cm−1 correspond to NH and OH stretching vibrations, indicating the presence of hydroxyl and amine groups. These functional groups enhance solubility, stabilize CQDs in aqueous environments, and provide reactive sites for further functionalization. A strong peak at 1632 cm−1 is attributed to CO stretching, confirming the presence of carbonyl groups, which contribute to fluorescence quenching and energy transfer mechanisms.

The spectrum also exhibits peaks at 1138 cm−1 and 1412–1535 cm−1, associated with CO stretching and C–H/C–N vibrations, respectively, reflecting oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functionalities (Liu et al., 2021; Pomal et al., 2021). Incorporating phosphorus introduces additional characteristic peaks at 1150 cm−1 and 890 cm−1, attributed to PO and PO stretching vibrations, confirming the presence of phosphate groups. These phosphorus-containing species modulate electronic density, enhance charge transfer properties, and contribute to improved photoluminescence efficiency. Overall, the presence of hydroxyl, carbonyl, and phosphate groups confirms successful N,P co-doping, which significantly enhances the chemical reactivity, fluorescence properties, and potential applications of N,P-CQDs in bioimaging, sensing, and catalysis-related research (John et al., 2021; Shamsipur et al., 2018).

Fig. 5a shows that the emission FL spectra of N,P-CQDs are wavelength-dependent, varying in intensity while excited within the range of 270 and 340 nm. A maximum emission intensity of 676 nm is obtained with 300 nm excitation. These variations arise from the heterogeneous surface functionality and defects created during the synthesis steps, which trap energy as quantum dots, resulting in their photophysical properties (Jin et al., 2019; Liang et al., 2022). Other effects like particle size, presence of heteroatoms, and surface chemistry can also decisively influence the FL property (Singh et al., 2020; Tian & Yin, 2019; Zhao et al., 2018).

Fig. 5b: UV–vis absorption spectra. Absorption at 195 nm and 214 nm peaks corresponds to polycyclic aromatic structures. When exposed to 365 nm UV light, N,P-CQDs have a strong blue fluorescence (inset of Fig. 5b). The QY of the prepared N,P-CQDs was 36.65 % (Text S1 and Figure S1), calculated relative to quinine sulfate with a QY of 54 % (Eaton, 1988; Würth et al., 2013). The QY of the prepared N,P-CQDs was 36.65 % (Text S1 and Figure S1), calculated relative to quinine sulfate with a QY of 54 % (Eaton, 1988; Würth et al., 2013). This value surpasses those reported (Table S1) for other biomass-derived CQDs (Chandra et al., 2022; Lai et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2018; Mei et al., 2024; Qi et al., 2019; Wang, Yang, et al., 2023). The enhanced QY can be attributed to nitrogen and phosphorus doping, which improved surface passivation, increased electron density, and enhanced radiative recombination. The hydrothermal method ensured controlled particle size (6.01 nm), enhancing quantum confinement effects and minimizing non-radiative recombination losses.

The stability studies over time of N,P-CQDs were assessed by analyzing fluorescence intensity at 300 nm after 80 days of storage at 5 °C (Fig. 6a). The results showed only a 4.8 % decrease in fluorescence intensity, demonstrating the strong stability of N,P-CQDs. This exceptional stability is attributed to the presence of phosphorus-derived functional groups such as P-O-C and PO bonds, which effectively passivate the CQD surface, reducing surface defects and minimizing non-radiative recombination pathways. Such stabilization mechanisms have been reported to enhance the structural rigidity of CQDs, thereby improving their long-term stability in storage conditions (Zhi et al., 2018). In addition, the fluorescence intensity fluctuations during continuous UV irradiation at 365 nm for 30 min stayed within 2 % variance, indicating excellent resistance to photobleaching (Fig. 6d). This enhanced photostability is likely due to phosphorus atoms stabilizing the carbon framework, preventing photo-induced degradation by inhibiting defect-driven energy dissipation. The pH-dependent FL properties were studied by changing the pH from 3 to 10. Fig. 6b shows that the maximum FL intensity occurs at pH 7, with a noticeable decrease under acidic (pH 3–6) and basic (pH 8–10) conditions. These changes can be attributed to the protonation and deprotonation of surface functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and phosphate groups in the presence of H+ or OH− ions, which alter the electronic structure and affect fluorescence emission (Kayani et al., 2024). The presence of stable phosphorus-based functional groups may also contribute to buffering surface charge changes, maintaining fluorescence intensity across a broader pH range. Furthermore, to evaluate the effect of ionic strength, the FL intensity was measured in KCl solutions with concentrations ranging from 0 to 2 M. Fig. 6c displays no significant changes in FL intensity, demonstrating that ionic strength variations have minimal impact on N,P-CQD fluorescence. This behavior suggests that the surface functional groups are tightly anchored, preventing aggregation or disruption under high ionic strength conditions, making N,P-CQDs suitable for complex environmental and biological samples (Chatzimitakos et al., 2023). Collectively, these findings confirm the outstanding stability, photostability, and environmental robustness of N,P-CQDs, reinforcing their suitability for long-term sensing applications in diverse matrices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Testing specificity and quenching of fluorescence

ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence quenching efficiency of N,P-CQDs was optimized for incubation time and pH (Fig. 7 ). Results show no significant impact of these parameters on quenching efficiency, allowing experiments to be conducted at pH 7 with a 10-min incubation time. By equilibrating N,P-CQDs with different plasticizers under 300 nm excitation at a concentration of 25 μM, selectivity studies were achieved. Fig. 7a highlights drastic FL quenching, whereas dibutyl phthalate has shown more significant inhibition towards other plasticizers such as diethyl phthalate (DEP), butyl benzyl phthalate (BBP), bis(2-ethylhexyl) 3,4,5,6-tetrabromo phthalate (TBPH), polyethylene glycol (PEG), and phthalic anhydride (PA). Quenching ratios of F0/F were measured at a DBP concentration range of 1–25 μM to quantify the DBP detection. A linear response was observed and demonstrated in Fig. 7b with a regression equation for a high correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.99704), shown in Fig. 7c. The constant, Stern-Volmer Ksᵥ, is estimated as 0.01825. LOD was found to be calculated by the formula:
(1)
LOD = 3 σ / m
where σ represents the standard deviation of the y-intercept and m depicts the slope of the calibration curve. As a result, the LOD for DBP was determined to be 0.632 μM, which implies that N,P-CQDs are quite sensitive at detecting DBP in different matrices.

The selectivity of the N,P-CQDs fluorescence sensor for DBP was assessed by introducing other analytes (DEP, BBP, TBPH, PEG, and PA) as potential interferents. As shown in Figure S3, the fluorescence quenching remained unchanged upon adding these analytes, confirming that the sensor selectively responds to DBP. The minimal impact of other analytes suggests that the quenching mechanism is highly specific, likely due to the strong interactions between DBP and the functional groups on the N,P-CQD surface. These findings demonstrate the high specificity of the developed sensor, reinforcing its reliability for DBP detection in real-world applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The interaction of DBP with N,P-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction mechanism between DBP and N,P-CQDs was examined using FTIR studies, UV–vis spectroscopy, and fluorescence lifetime analysis. FTIR spectra exhibited notable shifts in the -OH and -NH stretching vibrations about 3400 cm−1, indicating hydrogen bonding interactions between DBP and N,P-CQDs. Furthermore, modifications in the CC and CO stretching bands around 1650 cm−1 signified the existence of π-π stacking and electrostatic interactions, demonstrating that DBP predominantly engages with the surface functional groups of N,P-CQDs (Fig. 8a). The observed weakening of these functional groups suggests the formation of hydrogen bonds between the carbonyl groups of DBP and the amine or hydroxyl groups on the N,P-CQD surface. Such interactions facilitate electron transfer, promoting dynamic quenching. UV–Vis spectroscopy provided key evidence for the IFE by demonstrating significant spectral overlap between the absorption spectrum of DBP and the excitation/emission wavelengths of N,P-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 8b, the characteristic absorption peak of DBP overlaps with the excitation wavelength of N,P-CQDs, leading to reduced fluorescence intensity due to light absorption by DBP before it can excite the quantum dots. The disappearance of the DBP peak in the presence of N,P-CQDs further supports this interaction, indicating that fluorescence quenching occurs through IFE rather than chemical degradation. Additionally, the absorbance spectra before and after DBP addition confirm that the quenching is driven by photophysical interference rather than direct molecular interactions. In conjunction with fluorescence lifetime analysis, these findings substantiate IFE as a major contributor to the observed fluorescence quenching mechanism.

A Stern-Volmer plot was constructed to quantitatively assess the fluorescence quenching behavior of N,P-CQDs in the presence of DBP, revealing a linear correlation between fluorescence intensity (F0/F) and DBP concentration (Fig. 7c). The Stern-Volmer constant (Ksv) was determined to be 1.825 × 104 M−1, indicating strong quenching efficiency. The bimolecular quenching rate constant Kq, calculated using Kq = Ksv/T0, was 3.16 × 1012 M−1 s−1, where Ksv is Stern-Volmer constant and T0 is the fluorescence lifetime in the absence of a quencher, confirming a dynamic quenching mechanism consistent with the diffusion-controlled limit. Furthermore, the LOD was determined to be 0.632 μM, underscoring the high sensitivity of the N,P-CQDs towards DBP detection. The exceptional linearity (R2 = 0.99704) further validates the reliability and precision of the developed fluorescence sensing platform. The fluorescence QY was measured before and after DBP addition to evaluate environmental stability (Figure S1). Despite only an 8 % reduction in QY, the fluorescence intensity exhibited a significant decline in the presence of DBP. This pronounced decrease can be attributed to the combined effects of the IFE and dynamic quenching mechanisms, which reduce emission intensity without heavily impacting the material's inherent efficiency. Additionally, zeta potential analysis demonstrated a shift from −23.5 mV to −15.2 mV after DBP exposure, confirming the influence of electrostatic interactions during the quenching process (Figure S2). To further investigate the quenching mechanism, fluorescence lifetime measurements using counting (TCSPC) revealed a minor reduction in the fluorescence lifespan of N,P-CQDs from 5.77 ns to 5.64 ns following exposure to DBP (Table S2, Fig. 8c). This reduction aligns with a dynamic quenching mechanism, confirming electron transfer involvement. Hydrogen bonding, π-π stacking, and intersystem crossing interactions collectively enhance the quenching efficiency.

The hypothesized quenching mechanism, illustrated in Fig. 8d, indicates that DBP interacts with the amine, hydroxyl, and phosphoryl groups on the surface of N,P-CQDs, establishing a conduit for electron transport that facilitates dynamic quenching. Additionally, forming a ground-state complex, enhanced π-π stacking interactions and efficient energy transfer pathways collectively contribute to the overall quenching behavior. The synergistic effects of IFE and dynamic quenching highlight the exceptional sensitivity of N,P-CQDs for DBP detection, yielding a fluorescence response characterized by diminished intensity and reduced lifespan.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of DBP recovery in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practical applicability and selectivity of N,P-CQDs for detecting DBP, recovery experiments were conducted using plastic-packaged food items, including milk, cheese, and yogurt. These samples were obtained from a local milk booth and processed according to the standardized protocol described earlier. The fluorescence response of N,P-CQDs in these complex food matrices was studied by spiking DBP at 5, 10, and 15 μM concentrations, followed by fluorescence-based quantification. Figure S4(a-c) illustrates the fluorescence quenching behavior of N,P-CQDs in milk, cheese, and yogurt upon interaction with increasing DBP concentrations (5, 10, and 15 μM). A gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed with rising DBP levels, indicating strong interaction between DBP and N,P-CQDs across all food matrices. The quenching effect can be attributed to the IFE, where DBP absorbs either the excitation or emitted light, and dynamic quenching, where DBP facilitates electron transfer with the excited N,P-CQDs, leading to fluorescence suppression.

The calibration graph (Figure S4d) for DBP determination in real samples exhibited a linear response over the tested concentration range, demonstrating high correlation coefficients (R2) of 0.99965 for milk, 0.99562 for cheese, and 0.99455 for yogurt, indicating strong linearity and reliable quantification across different matrices. The calculated LOD values were 0.596 μM for milk, 2.422 μM for cheese, and 2.111 μM for yogurt, suggesting that the method exhibits the highest sensitivity in milk samples, followed by yogurt and cheese. The variations in LOD across different matrices can be attributed to differences in their fat, protein, and water content, which influence DBP-N,P-CQD interactions. Milk, a liquid with lower fat content and higher water content, allows better dispersion of N,P-CQDs and facilitates efficient quenching, leading to the lowest LOD (0.596 μM). Yogurt, with a higher viscosity and protein content due to fermentation, may cause partial aggregation or hinder free diffusion of DBP molecules, slightly increasing the LOD (2.111 μM). Cheese, having the highest fat content among the tested matrices, introduces hydrophobic interactions that can partially restrict DBP accessibility to N,P-CQDs, further increasing the LOD (2.422 μM). These results indicate that sample composition significantly affects the detection sensitivity, with aqueous environments favoring better sensor performance.

Recovery rates for DBP detection in real samples ranged from 97.60 % to 101.67 %, with relative standard deviations (RSDs) between 5.97 % and 9.96 % (Table 1 ). These results confirm the high accuracy and reproducibility of the fluorescence-based method in complex food matrices. The minor variations in RSD values indicate good precision while reflecting slight differences in sample composition and matrix effects. Notably, milk samples showed relatively stable fluorescence signals, whereas yogurt exhibited slightly higher deviations, possibly due to variations in fat and protein content affecting DBP-N,P-CQD interactions. The strong fluorescence response, high recovery efficiency, and reliable quantification results demonstrate that N,P-CQDs offer a robust and sensitive method for DBP detection in real-world food samples. This method outperforms conventional analytical techniques by providing rapid, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly detection of plasticizers in packaged food products. The minimal interference from matrix components further highlights the robustness of this sensing approach, making it suitable for large-scale food safety monitoring and regulatory applications.

Our study demonstrates that N,P co-doped CQDs synthesized from Phyllanthus emblica effectively detect DBP in dairy products like packaged milk, cheese, and yogurt, with a detection limit of 0.632 μM and an operational range of 1–25 μM. This fluorescence-based method surpasses current detection techniques in simplicity and biocompatibility. Unlike conventional colorimetric and electrochemical sensors, which rely on complex processes, costly materials, or enzyme-based reactions requiring multi-step sample pretreatments, our approach enables rapid, non-invasive, and eco-friendly detection (Table 2 ) (Wang, Pan, Liu, et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2018). Techniques such as the Pt@Au nanozyme assay and molecularly imprinted polypyrrole sensors, though efficient, have limited detection sensitivity (typically above 0.5 μg/L) and are less practical for real-time food safety monitoring (Li, Zhang, et al., 2023). By employing fluorescence quenching via an electron transfer mechanism, our N,P-CQDs offer a more accessible and scalable solution, performing better than previously reported PV-CQDs prepared from Phaseolus vulgaris for cookie detection (Thakur, Bains, Sridhar, et al., 2024), which had a higher detection limit (3.19634 μM) and a narrower range (0.1–10 μM). The green synthesis using Phyllanthus emblica contributes to the sensor's eco-friendliness and biocompatibility, highlighting its potential for scalable, on-site food safety applications with enhanced sensitivity and ease of use.

This study presents a sustainable fluorescence-based detection platform utilizing N,P-CQDs synthesized from Phyllanthus emblica for the sensitive and selective detection of the food packaging plasticizer DBP. The green synthesis approach employed an abundant natural precursor, yielding 6.05 nm N,P-CQDs with a QY of 36.65 %. The synthesized CQDs demonstrated stable fluorescence, excellent water dispersibility, and notable photostability under varying environmental conditions. The detection mechanism, driven by dynamic quenching via the inner filter effect, achieved a LOD of 0.632 μM. Furthermore, recovery rates ranging from 97.60 % to 101.67 % in complex food matrices such as milk, cheese, and yogurt confirmed the practical applicability of this sensing platform. The proposed fluorescence-based method offers significant advantages over conventional detection approaches, including simplicity, cost-effectiveness, and environmental sustainability. The green synthesis strategy emphasizes its potential as an eco-friendly alternative for food safety monitoring and environmental analysis. This study provides a foundation for extending the application of N,P-CQDs to detect other hazardous plasticizers and environmental contaminants. Future research may focus on enhancing the selectivity of N,P-CQDs for structurally similar analytes, expanding their utility across diverse food matrices and developing portable sensor platforms for real-time, on-site monitoring. Such advancements could further improve this method's practicality and commercial viability for ensuring food safety and environmental protection.
